exemplar.title:
Traditional Notions and Evolving Societal Perceptions in 12 Angry Men: Given its
historical context, how does the play 12 Angry Men reflect the evolving societal perceptions of justice, and in what ways does it challenge or reinforce traditional notions of justice?

detailedExemplarData.title:
Traditional Notions and Evolving Societal Perceptions in 12 Angry Men: Given its
historical context, how does the play 12 Angry Men reflect the evolving societal perceptions of justice, and in what ways does it challenge or reinforce traditional notions of justice?

subject.title: English Lang & Lit (Old)
subject.description: IB English Language & Literature combines the study of language and literature, analyzing both literary and non-literary texts across various media and contexts. Students explore how language shapes meaning, identity, and culture through critical examination of diverse text types including advertisements, speeches, novels, and digital media. The course develops analytical skills, cultural understanding, and critical thinking about communication in different contexts and purposes.

strengths: The research question is stated clearly and drives the entire investigation, with section headings that mirror its wording.
Contextual framing (1950s demographics, civil-rights climate) is integrated seamlessly to maintain precise focus throughout.
Sub-headings and signposts reinforce purpose and guide the reader effectively.
weaknesses: A few typographical and phrasing lapses remain (e.g., incomplete sentence on page 3).
Lack of a brief methodological reflection section slightly reduces transparency of source selection.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the initial strand assessment placed this work at the top of the 5â6 band with a 5, the near-flawless clarity of purpose, consistent signposting, and articulate framing justify the full 6. The few typos and missing methodological commentary are minor and do not detract from the exceptional communication of topic, focus, and method.

strengths: Accurate integration of key secondary scholarship (Higginbotham, Ogletree) supports central claims.
Terminology (âreasonable doubt,â âgroupthink,â âimpartial juryâ) is used correctly and in context.
Coherent understanding of both the primary text and socio-historical background.
weaknesses: Several sources are non-academic (blogs, popular news), weakening scholarly rigor.
Some assertions remain surface-level and would benefit from deeper critical engagement.
A concise definition of legal terms (e.g., reasonable doubt) is occasionally omitted.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates good knowledge and generally effective use of sources, placing it firmly in the 3â4 band. Persistent reliance on non-peer-reviewed materials and occasional superficial treatment of concepts prevent a band-5 rating, so a score of 4 accurately reflects both strengths and areas for improvement.

strengths: Research is consistently relevant to the central question and draws on a diverse set of theoretical and historical sources.
Analysis of key scenes (Juror 8âs demonstration, Juror 10âs rant) is insightful and well supported by textual evidence.
The argument is organised clearly around challenge vs. reinforcement, with a balanced conclusion reflecting critical engagement.
weaknesses: Depth of analysis is uneven, with occasional drift into summary or extended historical exposition.
Engagement with counter-interpretations is minimal, limiting the sophistication of evaluation.
Methodological reflexivity and interrogation of source limitations are not fully developed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While initial strand scores fell in the 4â6 band (5,5,5), the sustained relevance of research, generally well-supported conclusions, and coherent critical evaluation warrant placement in the upper tier of the 10â12 band. The work falls short of an 11 or 12 because it lacks consistent analytical depth and counter-argument engagement, making 10 the most fitting holistic mark.

strengths: Complete and logical structure with a title page, table of contents, clearly labelled sections, and bibliography.
Transitions between sections are smooth, guiding the reader from introduction through conclusion.
Consistent application of IB presentation conventions.
weaknesses: The table of contents lacks page numbers, hindering navigation.
Minor typographical artefacts (extra spaces, unintended inline math tags) remain.
A few bibliography entries omit publication details or use inconsistent formatting.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation fully satisfies IB structure requirements and applies layout conventions effectively, meriting the top mark of 4. The noted technical lapses are small and do not compromise overall readability or coherence.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The student employs a primary source (Roseâs play) plus roughly fifteen secondary items ranging from scholarly books (Fuller, Young-Bruehl) to web articles and blogs.  Citation style is consistent and a full bibliography is supplied, indicating a mostly complete methodology.

Selection shows some informed choicesâlegal theory, historical civil-rights commentaryâbut the academic rigour of several sources (e.g., blog posts, generic psychology websites) is questionable, and there is little explicit methodological reflection on why particular texts were prioritised.  No separate section explains research procedures or evaluative criteria for source reliability.

Given the adequate but uneven range and only partial evidence of effective selection, the strand aligns with the middle (3-4) band; the upper value of 4 recognises that most sources remain relevant to the inquiry.

Formatting conventions (word count declaration, title, hierarchical headings, and a full bibliography) are in place, and typography is mostly consistent.  The extract suggests page breaks labelled {0}, {1}, etc., implying pagination is present, though the numbering system is unconventional.

There are several technical lapsesâextra spaces in URLs, occasional markdown artefacts, and minor typographic errorsâwhich indicate that layout conventions are not flawlessly executed.

Given that most but not all layout considerations are "present and correctly applied," the strand is best situated in the 3-4 band; a mark of 3 is awarded to reflect the noted inconsistencies.

Research is clearly relevant to the question: every secondary source addresses jury composition, legal theory, or prejudice, all of which inform the analysis of justice in the play.  The primary text is foregrounded, and historical examples (Scottsboro Boys) illuminate arguments.

Nonetheless, research depth is mixedâseveral sources lack academic authority, and there is no engagement with opposing scholarly interpretations.  These factors prevent placement in the highest (7-9) band.

Given predominantly appropriate but not consistently high-quality research, the strand aligns with the 4-6 band; a mid-band mark of 5 reflects sound relevance with some limitations.

The title and introductory paragraphs articulate a precise focus on the way 12 Angry Men mirrors and interrogates concepts of justice.  The student situates the text historically (1950s jury demographics, civil-rights climate) and immediately links that context to the guiding issue of evolving vs. traditional views of justice.  This clear framing is maintained by consistent section headings that echo the wording of the research question.

Because the purpose, scope, and direction are explicit and repeatedly signposted, the topic is communicated accurately and without ambiguity.  Minor lapses in phrasing (e.g., occasional typographical errors) do not impede the readerâs grasp of what is being investigated.

Overall the submission satisfies the descriptor for âcommunicated accurately and effectively,â hence placement in the 5-6 band, with a mid-band mark of 5 awarded.

The discussion is clearly organised around the dual lens of challenge vs. reinforcement, culminating in a balanced conclusion that revisits how the play both critiques and upholds justice.  Evaluation of the justice systemâs shortcomings (bias, lack of diversity) and its safeguards (evidence, moral duty) shows a degree of critical engagement.

Yet evaluation is sometimes asserted rather than rigorously demonstrated; the essay seldom weighs the relative significance of each point or interrogates source limitations, and reflective awareness of alternative viewpoints is limited.

Accordingly, the work fulfils the descriptor for âgood argument development with partially critical evaluation,â meriting a 5 within the 4-6 band.

The student offers structured analysis of key scenes (Juror 10âs racist speech, Juror 8âs demonstrations) and links these to broader theories (groupthink, reasonable doubt).  Claims are frequently supported with textual quotation and contextual evidence, and the essay moves beyond narration to interpretive commentary.

However, the analytical depth is uneven; some paragraphs drift into summary or historical exposition without fully returning to the central interpretive thread.  Counter-arguments are acknowledged only briefly, limiting the sophistication of the reasoning.

These strengths and shortcomings situate the work solidly within the âgood analysis / mostly supported conclusionsâ description, justifying a score of 5 in the 4-6 band.

The essay follows IB EE conventions: title page with word count, table of contents, clearly labelled sections, introduction, conclusion, and bibliography.  Logical sequencing (Introduction â Thematic Sections â Conflict â Conclusion) guides the reader effectively.

All required structural elements are present, and transitions between sections are generally smooth.  Minor issues such as an empty column in the contents table and occasional heading inconsistencies do not undermine overall clarity.

Because the structure is both complete and appropriate, the strand earns placement in the 3-4 band, with the higher mark of 4 given.

The essay displays solid knowledge of both the dramatic text and its socio-historical setting: the student recounts key plot points, quotes accurately, and integrates contextual details about 1950s juries and civil-rights legislation.  Secondary scholarship is generally incorporated to support claims (Higginbotham on exclusionary practices, Ogletree on minority representation).

Nevertheless, some assertions remain surface-level or rely on generalised historical summaries; a deeper engagement with literary criticism on Rose or more extensive legal scholarship would have strengthened coherence.  Source use is competent but not consistently analyticalâseveral references are cited mainly for illustrative purposes.

Consequently the work fits the âgood knowledge / clear understandingâ description, meriting a 4 within the 3-4 band.

Subject-specific languageâterms such as âreasonable doubt,â âgroupthink,â âprejudice,â and âimpartial juryââis used correctly, and the student references literary devices (exposition, dramatic tension, anonymity of jurors).  Legal and philosophical terminology is largely accurate.

There is, however, some inconsistency: typographical errors (e.g., extra punctuation, mis-hyphenation) and a few vague phrases (âtrue justice requires more legal formalitiesâ) detract from complete precision.  Conceptual definitions are sometimes asserted rather than thoroughly unpacked.

Overall terminology is mostly accurate and appropriate, satisfying the 3-4 descriptor at the top end; a score of 4 is awarded.

The research question is explicitly stated on the title page and is revisited in the introduction and conclusion, remaining the organising thread of the discussion.  Its wording is sufficiently narrow (one text, one theme, dual analytical lenses of challenge vs. reinforcement) to allow focused treatment within 4 000 words.

Throughout the body, sub-sections (e.g., âChallenging Traditional Notions of Justice,â âReinforcing Traditional Notionsâ) map directly onto the two parts of the question, demonstrating a tight connection between question and ensuing analysis.  Occasional digressions into broad historical anecdotes are present, yet they are generally tethered back to the core enquiry.

Because the question is clear, appropriately scoped, and effectively drives the essay, the work meets the 5-6 descriptor; a mark of 5 is justified to acknowledge a few moments where ancillary material threatens to broaden the focus.

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly stated and effectively frames the investigationâs focus on traditional versus evolving notions of justice.
The inclusion of a precise word count meets IB formatting requirements and demonstrates adherence to guidelines.
Consider shortening the title to improve readability; a more concise title will strengthen focus and immediately capture reader interest.
The table of contents is clearly presented but lacks page numbers, making navigation less efficient; add page references for each section.
Consider aligning the tableâs empty cells or removing unnecessary columns to improve clarity in the contents layout.
The opening paragraph provides a clear summary of plot events but relies heavily on narration; more analytical framing would strengthen the introduction.
The anecdote about King Morganâs jury origin lacks academic support and relies on a popular news source; replace with peer-reviewed scholarship.
The final sentence on this page is incomplete. Ensure each paragraph concludes fully to maintain clarity and cohesion.
This discussion of Platonic justice effectively contextualizes the play, but the paragraph would benefit from splitting at the transition to Roseâs dramaturgy.
The inline math tag around the dash appears unintended. Replace with a standard em-dash for stylistic consistency.
The section header cleanly signals the shift to societal perceptions and maintains focus on the research question.
The analysis of Juror 8âs moral stance is well supported by textual evidence, enhancing interpretive depth.
The paragraph drifts into historical exposition without consistently tying back to the central argument; tighten focus on how these contexts inform the playâs critique.
Citations such as âAct I on page 12â could be standardized to âAct I, p. 12â for consistent scholarly style.
Integration of Higginbothamâs research effectively supports the critique of exclusionary jury practices.
This dense historical-context paragraph would benefit from splitting to aid reader comprehension and highlight key arguments.
The discussion of Ogletreeâs argument on presumption of guilt is well integrated, adding scholarly weight.
Several sentences repeat ideas already stated; condense redundant phrasing to sharpen argument delivery.
Detailed quotations of Juror 10âs racist remarks effectively illustrate how prejudice undermines traditional justice.
The analysis relies heavily on Young-Bruehl; consider introducing counter-interpretations to deepen critical engagement.
Theoretical framing of groupthink is well executed, linking Janisâs findings to the playâs consensus dynamics.
You invoke reasonable doubt but do not define it; include a concise definition to clarify its legal significance.
The ethical duty argument in the reinforcement section powerfully synthesizes law and morality.
The reference âGloomy Facts, the Centrality of Lawâ lacks full bibliographic detail; provide author, date, and publisher.
The conclusion restates central insights effectively, though a brief methodological reflection would enrich the essayâs self-awareness.
The bibliography includes useful sources but features several non-academic blogs; prioritizing peer-reviewed material will improve research quality.
Several bibliography entries are missing publication years or consistent formatting; ensure each entry follows the chosen citation style.
URL line breaks disrupt readability; apply hanging indent formatting to bibliography links to maintain a clean layout.
